---
title: "Crime & Safety"
description: "Safety in Canada — from long-run severity down to the neighbourhood: trends, who solves what, and where it happens"
body-classes: area-people   # nav-grouped under People & Society (not the Health area of this directory)
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
import os, json
import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from pipeline.config import get_data_date, BRAND, CANADA_COLOR
from pipeline.charts import (choropleth_groups_map, category_bar, single_line, single_bar,
                             single_line_multi, lines_over_time_geo_select)

CRIME = "data/wellbeing/statcan_crime_by_type.csv"
d = pd.read_csv(CRIME, dtype={"cmauid": str})
LATEST = int(d["year"].max())
# Source notes are branded automatically by the Figure.show interceptor (charts.py);
# the one exception is the markdown caption under the clearance chart below.
SRC = lambda t=CRIME: f"Source: Statistics Canada, Table 35-10-0177-01 | Data as of: {get_data_date(t)}"
```

Safety is a basic part of well-being, and crime is usually discussed one incident
at a time. This page puts those incidents in context — from the long-run *severity*
of crime down to what is happening in a single neighbourhood. The two
authoritative sources are Statistics Canada's incident-based crime
statistics (the UCR) and, for the local view, the Toronto Police Service open-data
portal.

## The headline measures: severity and homicide

Two measures summarise the long-run picture. The **Crime Severity Index** weights
each offence by its seriousness (so a homicide counts far more than a minor theft)
and is indexed to 2006 = 100; the **homicide rate** is the most consistently measured
serious crime over the long run, reaching back to 1961. **Use the dropdown** to switch
between them (and drag the slider to see homicide's longer history). Everything after
this breaks the summary apart — by *type* of crime, by whether it gets solved, and by
*place*.

```{python}
#| echo: false
csi = pd.read_csv("data/wellbeing/statcan_crime_severity.csv", parse_dates=["date"])[["date", "crime_severity_index"]]
hom = pd.read_csv("data/wellbeing/statcan_homicide_rate.csv", parse_dates=["date"])[["date", "homicide_rate"]]
m = pd.merge(csi, hom, on="date", how="outer").sort_values("date")
csi_date = get_data_date("data/wellbeing/statcan_crime_severity.csv")
fig = single_line_multi(m, "date", options=[
    {"col": "crime_severity_index", "label": "Crime Severity Index",
     "yaxis_title": "Crime Severity Index (2006 = 100)", "hovertemplate": "%{y:.1f}<extra></extra>"},
    {"col": "homicide_rate", "label": "Homicide rate",
     "yaxis_title": "Homicides per 100,000 population", "hovertemplate": "%{y:.2f}<extra></extra>"}],
    source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada, Tables 35-10-0026-01 (CSI) & 35-10-0068-01 (homicide) | Data as of: {csi_date}")
# Open on the recent window (CSI starts 1998); the slider still spans homicide's 1961– history.
fig.update_xaxes(range=["1997-06-01", f"{LATEST}-12-31"])
fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
```

## What crime do people experience?

The headline index hides *composition* — a single number can't tell you whether a
place's issue is car theft, break-ins, assault, or fraud. These are the offences that
make up most of the crime people encounter — everyday property and disorder crime, the
three tiers of assault (**common**, **with a weapon or injury**, and **aggravated**),
and (far rarer) homicide — as **rates per 100,000 people** so places and years are
comparable. **Use the geography selector** to switch between Canada, any
province or territory, and any of the ~40 metropolitan areas (grouped, and searchable —
start typing a city name).

::: {#crime-experience}
```{python}
#| echo: false
from IPython.display import HTML, display

EVERYDAY_COLORS = {
    "Theft under $5,000": "#4e79a7", "Mischief": "#f28e2b",
    "Shoplifting": "#59a14f", "Fraud": "#e15759",
    "Breaking & entering": "#b07aa1", "Theft from a vehicle": "#76b7b2",
    "Motor vehicle theft": "#9c755f", "Robbery": "#edc948",
    "Common assault": "#d98cb6", "Assault with a weapon or injury": "#a23a7e",
    "Aggravated assault": "#5a2347", "Homicide": "#111111",
}
fig, controls = lines_over_time_geo_select(
    d, group_col="violation", value_col="rate", colors=EVERYDAY_COLORS,
    yaxis_title="Rate per 100,000 population", div_id="crime-experience",
    hover_unit=" per 100k", source_note=SRC(), rangeslider=True,
    title="Crime rates by type")
display(HTML(controls))
fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
```
:::

Two patterns stand out. The largest category by far is everyday **theft under
$5,000** — the bucket that would include things like package or "porch" theft (no
official statistic isolates parcel theft specifically). And **theft *from* a
vehicle** has fallen sharply over the decade even as **vehicle theft itself** and
**fraud** have climbed.

## What's rising, and what's falling?

Overall crime is well below its 1990s–2000s levels, but that headline hides very
different trajectories underneath it. This shows each offence's change in rate over
the last decade. **Use the dropdown to switch between Canada, the provinces, and the
ten largest cities.** Colour indicates direction only.

```{python}
#| echo: false
Y0, Y1 = 2014, LATEST
CHANGE_SET = ["Identity theft", "Fraud", "Shoplifting", "Assault with a weapon or injury",
              "Motor vehicle theft", "Common assault", "Violent crime (total)",
              "Aggravated assault", "Homicide", "Robbery", "Property crime (total)",
              "Mischief", "Theft under $5,000", "Breaking & entering", "Theft from a vehicle"]
# Top 10 CMAs by 2021-census population (stable ordering; exact data labels)
TOP_CMA = ["Toronto, Ontario [35535]", "Montréal, Quebec [24462]",
           "Vancouver, British Columbia [59933]", "Ottawa-Gatineau, Ontario/Quebec [24505/35505]",
           "Calgary, Alberta [48825]", "Edmonton, Alberta [48835]", "Québec, Quebec [24421]",
           "Winnipeg, Manitoba [46602]", "Hamilton, Ontario [35537]",
           "Kitchener-Cambridge-Waterloo, Ontario [35541]"]
_TERR = ("Yukon", "Northwest Territories", "Nunavut")
_provs = sorted(g for g in d[d["geo_level"] == "province"]["geography"].unique()
                if not any(t in g for t in _TERR))
_prov_label = lambda g: g.split(" [")[0]   # "Ontario [35]" -> "Ontario"
_city_label = lambda g: g.split(",")[0]    # "Toronto, Ontario [35535]" -> "Toronto"
GEOS = ([("Canada", "Canada")]
        + [(_prov_label(g), g) for g in _provs]
        + [(_city_label(g), g) for g in TOP_CMA])

def _change(geo):
    piv = d[d["geography"] == geo].pivot_table(index="violation", columns="year", values="rate")
    rows = []
    for v in CHANGE_SET:
        if v in piv.index and Y0 in piv.columns and Y1 in piv.columns:
            a, b = piv.loc[v, Y0], piv.loc[v, Y1]
            if pd.notna(a) and pd.notna(b) and a > 0:
                rows.append((v, (b / a - 1) * 100))
    rows.sort(key=lambda r: r[1])
    lbls = [r[0] for r in rows]; vls = [r[1] for r in rows]
    return lbls, vls, [f"{v:+.0f}%" for v in vls], ["#d4763a" if v >= 0 else "#5a8fc2" for v in vls]

l0, v0, t0, c0 = _change("Canada")
fig = go.Figure(go.Bar(x=v0, y=l0, orientation="h", marker_color=c0,
    text=t0, textposition="auto", cliponaxis=False,
    hovertemplate="%{y}: %{x:+.0f}%<extra></extra>"))
buttons = []
for disp, geo in GEOS:
    lbls, vls, txt, cols = _change(geo)
    buttons.append(dict(label=disp, method="update",
        args=[{"x": [vls], "y": [lbls], "text": [txt], "marker.color": [cols]},
              {"yaxis.categoryorder": "array", "yaxis.categoryarray": lbls}]))
fig.update_layout(
    plot_bgcolor="white", height=560, showlegend=False,
    xaxis=dict(title=f"Change in rate per 100,000, {Y0}→{Y1}", gridcolor="#e0e0e0",
               ticksuffix="%", zeroline=True, zerolinecolor="#888", zerolinewidth=1.5),
    yaxis=dict(title="", categoryorder="array", categoryarray=l0),
    margin=dict(l=10, r=30, t=72, b=100),
    updatemenus=[dict(buttons=buttons, x=0, xanchor="left", y=1.13, yanchor="top",
                      direction="down", showactive=True)])
fig.add_annotation(text=SRC(), xref="paper", yref="paper", x=1, xanchor="right",
                   y=-0.28, showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"), align="right")
fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
```

**Fraud and identity theft have roughly doubled; shoplifting is up about two-thirds**
— the categories driving public anxiety — while traditional property crimes like
break-and-enter and theft from vehicles have fallen substantially. Among violent
offences, **assault involving a weapon or injury has climbed about three times faster
than common assault**. This split is why "is crime up or down?" has no single answer.

## Hate crime

Police-reported **hate crime** — criminal incidents motivated by hatred of a group's
race or ethnicity, religion, sexual orientation, or other identity — has risen far
faster than crime overall. It roughly **tripled since 2014**, with sharp jumps in
2020 and again in 2023.

```{python}
#| echo: false
hc_path = "data/wellbeing/statcan_hate_crime.csv"
hc = pd.read_csv(hc_path)
hc["date"] = pd.to_datetime(hc["year"], format="%Y")
fig = single_bar(hc, "date", "rate", title="",
    yaxis_title="Hate-crime incidents per 100,000", color=CANADA_COLOR,
    hovertemplate="%{y:.1f} per 100k<extra></extra>",
    source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada, Table 35-10-0191-01 | Data as of: {get_data_date(hc_path)}")
fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
```

Police figures **undercount** hate crime — most is never reported to police — and
rising counts partly reflect greater reporting and awareness, not only more
incidents. The bar below shows the communities targeted **per capita** — incidents
relative to each community's size, so a small community's experience isn't dwarfed by
a larger one's. (Use the toggle to switch to raw incident counts.)

```{python}
#| echo: false
hm_path = "data/wellbeing/statcan_hate_crime_motivation.csv"
hm = pd.read_csv(hm_path)
HM_LATEST = int(hm["year"].max())
COMMUNITIES = {
    "Jewish": "Jewish", "Black": "Black",
    "Sexual orientation": "Sexual orientation",
    "Arab (West Asian, Middle Eastern and North African origins)": "Arab / West Asian",
    "Muslim (Islam)": "Muslim", "South Asian": "South Asian",
    "East or Southeast Asian": "East / Southeast Asian",
    "Catholic": "Catholic", "Indigenous": "Indigenous",
}
# 2021 Census community populations, for the per-capita view. Total population except
# sexual orientation (no census count → StatCan survey estimate, ~1M aged 15+, flagged).
# Religion: 98-10-0353 (Jewish/Muslim/Catholic). Visible minority: 98-10-0324 (Black,
# South Asian; Arab+West Asian and the East/SE-Asian groups aggregated to match the
# police categories). Indigenous identity: 98-10-0264. Refresh at the 2026 Census.
POP_2021 = {
    "Jewish": 335295, "Muslim (Islam)": 1775715, "Catholic": 10880355,
    "Black": 1547865, "South Asian": 2571400,
    "Arab (West Asian, Middle Eastern and North African origins)": 694020 + 360490,
    "East or Southeast Asian": 1715775 + 218135 + 98895 + 957355 + 390340,
    "Indigenous": 1807250, "Sexual orientation": 1000000,
}
sub = hm[(hm["year"] == HM_LATEST) & (hm["motivation"].isin(COMMUNITIES))].copy()
sub["community"] = sub["motivation"].map(COMMUNITIES)
sub["rate"] = sub["count"] / sub["motivation"].map(POP_2021) * 100000

def _view(col):
    s = sub.sort_values(col)            # ascending → largest bar on top
    return s["community"].tolist(), s[col].tolist()
cy, cx = _view("count")
ry, rx = _view("rate")
INC_TITLE = f"Police-reported hate-crime incidents, {HM_LATEST}"
RATE_TITLE = "Incidents per 100,000 people of that community"
btn_rate = dict(method="update", label="Per 100,000 of community",
    args=[{"x": [rx], "y": [ry], "text": [[f"{v:,.1f}" for v in rx]],
           "hovertemplate": "%{y}: %{x:,.1f} per 100,000<extra></extra>"},
          {"xaxis.title.text": RATE_TITLE, "yaxis.categoryarray": ry, "xaxis.autorange": True}])
btn_count = dict(method="update", label="Number of incidents",
    args=[{"x": [cx], "y": [cy], "text": [[f"{v:,.0f}" for v in cx]],
           "hovertemplate": "%{y}: %{x:,.0f} incidents<extra></extra>"},
          {"xaxis.title.text": INC_TITLE, "yaxis.categoryarray": cy, "xaxis.autorange": True}])
# Opens on the per-capita view (the fairer comparison); toggle for raw counts.
fig = go.Figure(go.Bar(x=rx, y=ry, orientation="h", marker_color="#8c6bb1",
    text=[f"{v:,.1f}" for v in rx], textposition="auto", cliponaxis=False,
    hovertemplate="%{y}: %{x:,.1f} per 100,000<extra></extra>"))
fig.update_layout(
    plot_bgcolor="white", showlegend=False, height=170 + 30 * len(sub),
    xaxis=dict(title=RATE_TITLE, gridcolor="#e0e0e0"),
    yaxis=dict(title="", categoryorder="array", categoryarray=ry),
    margin=dict(l=10, r=70, t=54, b=120),
    updatemenus=[dict(active=0, x=0, xanchor="left", y=1.0, yanchor="bottom",
        bgcolor="white", bordercolor="#ccc", borderwidth=1, showactive=True,
        buttons=[btn_rate, btn_count])])
fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
```

::: {style="font-size:0.8em; color:#999; margin-top:0.3em;"}
Source: Statistics Canada — hate crime: Table 35-10-0066-01 (selected police services, most of Canada); community populations for the per-capita view: 2021 Census (Tables 98-10-0353 / 0324 / 0264; the sexual-orientation denominator is a StatCan survey estimate, not a census count). Police motivation categories approximate, but don't exactly match, census groups.<br>`{python} BRAND`
:::

Jewish and Black Canadians record the most incidents in raw numbers — but **per
capita the picture is starker**: relative to population, the Jewish community is
targeted far more than any other group, roughly five times the next-highest rate.
Incidents targeting Jewish people more than tripled from 2019, and the 2020–21
increase fell heaviest on East and Southeast Asian communities. (The breakdown is
from police services covering most, but not all, of Canada, so totals differ slightly
from the national series above.)

## How often does a crime get solved?

A "cleared" crime is one police have resolved — most often by laying a charge. The
clearance rate is the share of reported incidents that get cleared, and it differs
enormously by offence: violent crimes, where victims often know the offender, are
usually solved; high-volume property crimes mostly are not.

```{python}
#| echo: false
nl = d[(d["geo_level"] == "national") & (d["year"] == LATEST)].copy()
nl["clearance"] = nl["cleared"] / nl["incidents"] * 100
CLR = ["Homicide", "Aggravated assault", "Assault with a weapon or injury",
       "Violent crime (total)", "Common assault", "Robbery",
       "Shoplifting", "Property crime (total)", "Mischief", "Breaking & entering",
       "Fraud", "Theft under $5,000", "Theft from a vehicle", "Motor vehicle theft"]
cb = nl[nl["violation"].isin(CLR)][["violation", "clearance"]]
fig = category_bar(cb, "violation", "clearance",
                   xaxis_title=f"Share of reported incidents cleared, {LATEST}",
                   ticksuffix="%", value_fmt=".0f", color="#4e79a7")
fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
```

::: {style="font-size:0.8em; color:#999; margin-top:0.3em;"}
`{python} SRC()`<br>`{python} BRAND`
:::

Only about **1 in 10 reported vehicle thefts** and a similar share of thefts-from-
vehicles are cleared, versus more than half of violent crimes — and clearance **rises
with severity** (aggravated assaults are solved far more often than common ones).

## How metro areas compare

Crime across Canada's ~40 largest metropolitan areas for the latest year
(`{python} LATEST`). **Use the dropdown to change the measure** — it opens on the
overall **Crime Severity Index** (the severity-weighted summary, an index), then
breaks apart into specific offence rates per 100,000. Each measure rescales to its
own range, so a city that ranks high on auto theft may not on break-ins. Zoom and
pan to your area. (Larger metros only.)

```{python}
#| echo: false
cma_geo = json.load(open("data/geo/cma_2021.geojson"))
cl = d[(d["geo_level"] == "cma") & (d["year"] == LATEST)]
wide = cl.pivot_table(index=["cmauid", "name"], columns="violation", values="rate").reset_index()
# Join the Crime Severity Index (a weighted index — a different unit from the rates) so
# it leads the dropdown as the overall-severity view, then the specific offences follow.
cma_ind = pd.read_csv("data/geo/statcan_cma_indicators.csv", dtype={"cmauid": str})
wide = wide.merge(cma_ind[["cmauid", "crime_severity"]].rename(columns={"crime_severity": "csi"}),
                  on="cmauid", how="left")
CMA_GROUPS = [
    ("csi", "Crime Severity Index", {"suffix": "", "fmt": ".1f"}),
    # Violent offences
    ("Violent crime (total)", "Violent crime"),
    ("Common assault", "Common assault"),
    ("Robbery", "Robbery"),
    ("Homicide", "Homicide", {"fmt": ".1f"}),
    # Property offences
    ("Property crime (total)", "Property crime"),
    ("Motor vehicle theft", "Auto theft"),
    ("Breaking & entering", "Break & enter"),
    ("Theft from a vehicle", "Theft from vehicle"),
    ("Fraud", "Fraud")]
fig = choropleth_groups_map(
    cma_geo, wide, "cmauid", CMA_GROUPS, name_col="name", colorscale="YlOrRd",
    breakdown=False, value_fmt=".0f", value_suffix=" per 100k",
    cbar_title="{label}", cbar_ticksuffix="", center={"lat": 56.0, "lon": -96.0}, zoom=2.6,
    source_note="Source: Statistics Canada, Tables 35-10-0026-01 (Crime Severity Index) "
                "& 35-10-0177-01 (offence rates, per 100,000), latest year; larger metros only.")
fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "scrollZoom": True,
                 "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
```

Auto theft clusters in the Prairie metros and the Toronto–Hamilton corridor, while
violent crime, break-and-enter, and theft-from-vehicle each have different
geographies again. A single "crime ranking" of cities hides these very different
stories — which is why the dropdown matters. (Homicide is shown as a rate too, but
rests on small annual counts in any one metro, so read it cautiously.)

## Toronto, neighbourhood by neighbourhood

For the everyday, local scale — the texture of crime in one's own area — the Toronto
Police Service publishes rates per 100,000 for each of the city's **158
neighbourhoods**. **Switch the offence with the dropdown**; hover any neighbourhood for
its rate and population. These are the Toronto Police's own categories and counting
rules — a *different* body from Statistics Canada — so this map isn't directly
comparable to the national and metro figures above; read it as a **local texture** on
its own terms, not on the same scale. ("Assault (all types)" here, for instance,
combines *every* assault offence — common, with a weapon, aggravated, and more — not
just the common-assault tier shown on the StatCan charts.)

```{python}
#| echo: false
tor_geo = json.load(open("data/geo/toronto_neighbourhoods.geojson"))
tor = pd.read_csv("data/geo/toronto_neighbourhood_crime.csv", dtype={"hood_id": str})
TOR_YEAR = int(tor["year"].iloc[0])
TOR_GROUPS = [("auto_theft", "Auto theft"), ("bike_theft", "Bike theft"),
              ("break_enter", "Break & enter"), ("theft_from_mv", "Theft from vehicle"),
              ("assault", "Assault (all types)"), ("robbery", "Robbery"),
              ("theft_over", "Theft over $5,000"), ("shooting", "Shootings & discharges"),
              ("homicide", "Homicide")]
fig = choropleth_groups_map(
    tor_geo, tor, "hood_id", TOR_GROUPS, name_col="name", colorscale="YlOrRd",
    breakdown=False, value_fmt=".0f", value_suffix=" per 100k", cbar_title="{label}",
    cbar_ticksuffix="", pop_col="population", center={"lat": 43.72, "lon": -79.38},
    zoom=9.3, height=620,
    source_note=f"Source: Toronto Police Service, Neighbourhood Crime Rates ({TOR_YEAR}). "
                "Contains information licensed under the Open Government Licence – Ontario.")
fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "scrollZoom": True,
                 "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
```

Rates per capita look different from raw counts: busy downtown and some inner-suburb
neighbourhoods rank high on theft and assault, while violent firearm incidents are
rarer and more dispersed. Note that police locations are approximate (offset to the
nearest intersection for privacy), so this is a neighbourhood-level picture, not an
address-level one.

## Is crime seasonal?

A common intuition is that crime climbs with the summer heat. Toronto's monthly data
says *mostly not* — most offences are spread fairly evenly across the year. The big
exception is **bike theft**, which tracks the cycling season: nearly **half** its
annual total falls in June–August. Assault is mildly summer-peaked; the rest are
close to flat. Each line below shows the share of that crime's year-round incidents
falling in each month (a perfectly even crime would sit on the 8.3% line).

```{python}
#| echo: false
sea = pd.read_csv("data/geo/toronto_crime_seasonality.csv")
piv = sea.pivot_table(index="month", columns="crime", values="count", aggfunc="sum")
pct = piv.div(piv.sum(axis=0), axis=1) * 100      # each crime's 12 months sum to 100%
MO = ["Jan", "Feb", "Mar", "Apr", "May", "Jun", "Jul", "Aug", "Sep", "Oct", "Nov", "Dec"]
SEA_COLORS = {"Bike theft": "#d62728", "Assault": "#4e79a7", "Theft from vehicle": "#76b7b2",
              "Auto theft": "#9c755f", "Break & enter": "#b07aa1", "Robbery": "#edc948"}
fig = go.Figure()
for c in ["Bike theft", "Assault", "Theft from vehicle", "Auto theft", "Break & enter", "Robbery"]:
    if c in pct.columns:
        thick = c == "Bike theft"
        fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
            x=MO, y=[pct[c].get(m + 1) for m in range(12)], name=c, mode="lines+markers",
            line=dict(color=SEA_COLORS[c], width=3.5 if thick else 1.6),
            marker=dict(size=7 if thick else 4),
            hovertemplate=f"{c}, %{{x}}: %{{y:.1f}}%<extra></extra>"))
fig.add_hline(y=100 / 12, line_dash="dot", line_color="#aaa", opacity=0.8)
fig.update_layout(
    plot_bgcolor="white", height=460, hovermode="x unified",
    xaxis=dict(title="", gridcolor="#e0e0e0"),
    yaxis=dict(title="Share of the year's incidents", gridcolor="#e0e0e0", ticksuffix="%"),
    legend=dict(orientation="v", yanchor="top", y=1, xanchor="left", x=1.02),
    margin=dict(l=10, r=160, t=20, b=120))
fig.add_annotation(
    text="Source: Toronto Police Service open data (occurrences 2014–, by month). Each crime's 12 months sum to 100%.",
    xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, xanchor="left", y=-0.16, showarrow=False,
    font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))
fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})

bt = pd.read_csv("data/geo/toronto_bike_thefts.csv")
st = bt[bt["view"] == "status"]
BIKE_REC = f"{int(st[st['label'].astype(str).str.upper() == 'RECOVERED']['count'].sum()) / int(st['count'].sum()) * 100:.0f}"
```

So "more crime in summer" turns out to be largely a **bike-theft story** (and, to a
lesser degree, assault). Bike theft also illustrates the low-clearance pattern: of
the nearly 40,000 reported in Toronto since 2014, only about **`{python} BIKE_REC`%**
are ever recovered.

## Download data

- [Crime by type — Canada/provinces/metros (CSV)](../data/wellbeing/statcan_crime_by_type.csv) — Statistics Canada Table 35-10-0177-01
- [Crime Severity Index (CSV)](../data/wellbeing/statcan_crime_severity.csv) — Statistics Canada Table 35-10-0026-01
- [Homicide rate (CSV)](../data/wellbeing/statcan_homicide_rate.csv) — Statistics Canada Table 35-10-0068-01
- [Hate crime — national rate (CSV)](../data/wellbeing/statcan_hate_crime.csv) — Statistics Canada Table 35-10-0191-01
- [Hate crime — by motivation (CSV)](../data/wellbeing/statcan_hate_crime_motivation.csv) — Statistics Canada Table 35-10-0066-01
- [City indicators incl. Crime Severity (CSV)](../data/geo/statcan_cma_indicators.csv) — Statistics Canada (2021 Census + Table 35-10-0026-01)
- [Toronto neighbourhood crime rates (CSV)](../data/geo/toronto_neighbourhood_crime.csv) — Toronto Police Service (OGL – Ontario)
- [Toronto bicycle thefts summary (CSV)](../data/geo/toronto_bike_thefts.csv) — Toronto Police Service (OGL – Ontario)
- [Toronto crime seasonality by month (CSV)](../data/geo/toronto_crime_seasonality.csv) — Toronto Police Service (OGL – Ontario)
